data.title: How Stocks Might React After A Red-Hot March CPI Report
data.description:
On April 10, 2026, the Bureau of Labor Statistics will publish the Consumer Price Index for March. In February, the CPI increased 0.3% (seasonally adjusted), after it increased by 0.2% in January. A linear pattern would suggest that CPI in March increased 0.4%. Investors consider the median CPI and the longer-term trend in the last decade first.

February Median CPI

The Cleveland Fed calculated the February median CPI at 2.85%, compared to 2.41% in the last 12 months. The median CPI calculation is the one-month inflation rate of the component where the expenditure weight is in the 50th percentile of price changes. The rationale for omitting the outliers allows the reader to focus on the interior of the distribution of price changes.

The chart below shows a smoother long-term trend. The median CPI fell steadily since 2023, while the CPI (green line) exhibited more volatility, especially between 2024 and 2025. Despite the wider monthly changes, the CPI trended lower during that time.

Oil Price Shock in March

The bar chart below shows that the one-month percent change in CPI barely changed. CPI rose by either 0.2% or 0.3%. On one occasion last May 2025, CPI increased 0.1%.

In March, the BLS data will report the impact of surging oil prices. WTI crude (CL1:COM) rose from the low $70s. It stayed at elevated levels in the $90 - $98 range for most of the month.

The index for energy already increased 0.6% in February, compared to a fall of 1.5% in January. This figure might increase to 0.6% - 0.9%. Inflation in shelter, food, medical care, and apparel might lead to an overall increase in CPI. The CPI for the last 12 months might top 3.0%. Stock markets already priced in a 97.4% chance that the Federal Reserve will hold interest rates at 350 – 375 basis points.

The odds of a rate cut to 325-350 bps were higher on February 27 at 23.3%. Not only is a rate cut probability at 0%, but the odds of a rate hike are 2.6% at the time of writing this article.

The long-term treasury bill ETF (TLT) fell by 5% in the last month.

Investors should consider buying short-term treasury bills like the 0-3 Month (SGOV) for a 3.99% yield. iShares Treasury Floating Rate Bond ETF (TFLO) offers a higher yield at 4.05%. Capital loss risks from T-bills in the 1-3 year (SHY) or the 7-10 year maturity (IEF) are minimal. They dropped by 0-51% and 0.34% in the last year, respectively.

Avoid Retail Stocks

Oil prices are not likely to fall below $90/bbl in the next three months. The shutdown of the Strait of Hormuz and damage to energy facilities in the Middle East will keep energy prices higher for longer. Consumers now pay more than $4.00 per gallon, a price not seen in around four years. Lower disposable income will hurt demand for goods that consumers do not “must have.”

Nike (NKE) is an example of an apparel supplier suffering from weak sales and high inventory levels. Although weak sales in China and changes in consumer behavior are company-specific issues, the weak outlook sent NKE stock down by 15.51% on Wednesday.

Investors should also avoid Nike’s peers, including Lululemon (LULU), On Holdings (ONON), and Deckers Outdoor (DECK). In the quant ratings below, ONON stock has a Seeking Alpha “Sell” rating. It also scores the worst in the industry rank. Nike is ranked fourth, but the momentum scores of D+ indicate a low chance of a stock rebound.

Ralph Lauren (RL) scores an A- in profitability, momentum, and EPS revisions. RL stock is worth adding to the watch list.

Automotive companies will likely face a slump in sales. Ford (F), General Motors (G), Rivian (RIVN), Stellantis (STLA), and Tesla (TSLA) lost money for investors in 2026.

Ford and GM shares have a strong buy rating.

The two American automakers scored an A+ on profitability:

The index for medical care rose 0.5% in the last year. The aging U.S. population will increase demand for medical services. Unfortunately, medical care firms like Medical Properties Trust (MPT) are not attractive holdings. AH Realty Trust (AHRT) and Chiron Real Estate (XRN) are its peers. All three firms had negative returns YTD.

Food Inflation

Higher energy costs will increase operating expenditures for food manufacturers. Investors should continue to hold an overweight position in consumer staples companies. In particular, PepsiCo (PEP), Coca-Cola (KO), and the Procter & Gamble Co. (PG) have strong branding power. Shares in those firms are positive YTD:

Above: Keurig Dr Pepper (KDP) lost around 8%, while Celsius (CELH) fell by 25%. Discount retail firms are appealing to struggling consumers. Yet Dollar Tree (DLTR) shares fell by 11.6%. The quant factor grades are red (a warning) for PG, DLTR, and PEP shares. Coke stock edges ahead of Pepsi, scoring an A+ on growth.

Your Takeaway

Stock markets have already fallen sharply since peaking in early February, before the Iran invasion. The Nasdaq (QQQ), Dow Jones (DJI), and S&P 500 (SPY) neared correction territory (-10%) before staging a rally. QQQ stock, for example, gained over 3% on Tuesday.

The U.S. might signal a desire to end its conflict in Iran. However, Iran has firm control of the Strait of Hormuz. This will likely raise oil prices, where $90/bbl is the bottom and $100 - $120/bbl is the average.

Markets likely priced in a rise in the March CPI. It would limit the upside in the indices, especially the Russell 2000 (IWM). Small-cap firms typically struggle more amid a weaker economy.

data.title: Kura Oncology: Commercialization In AML Presents Asymmetric Upside
data.description:
In December 2024, I upgraded shares of Kura Oncology (KURA) to Strong Buy ahead of the company's FDA approval of Komzifti (ziftomenib). Then as now, Kura's commercialization remains approximately 6 months behind Syndax Pharma (SNDX). While I have advocated a basket approach, holding both stocks; the investment thesis for Kura is that its differentiated safety profile, avoiding a boxed warning for QTc prolongation, positions Kura to win market share in the large front-line Acute Myeloid Leukemia (1L AML) market in patients with NPM1 mutated or KMT2A-rearranged cancer. This approval remains years in the future, with the current FDA approval representing the smaller market of R/R NPM1-m AML.

Despite this $7B opportunity, Kura has lagged the overall market, returning -8% since I first upgraded around $9.12/share. With shares currently trading in the $8-range, the market is rewarding Syndax's early lead and doubting whether Kura's putative advantage will be sufficient to win market share. Kura's market cap of ~$715M remains deeply discounted, with a minuscule enterprise value when subtracting the company's strong liquidity of $667M in cash, cash equivalents and short-term investments. The company has a partnership with the Japanese firm Kyowa Kirin, wherein profits from the drug are split and Kyowa Kirin shoulders most of the costs and pays Kura royalties. This agreement caps upside, but puts Kura in a favorable financial position, booking 100% of US sales as revenue.

Last month, Kura reported Q4 2025 earnings booking $2.1M in sales for Komzifti in only the first 5 weeks following product launch. Thus, 2026 is an execution year: to outperform the stock will have to prove the market's pessimism overdone. However, analyst's estimates for 2026 revenue with the consensus at $111.4M seems very achievable. Importantly, Kura guided for 2026 milestone payments of $45M-$55M, thus the mid-point implies $61.4M in Komzifti sales, a run rate of $15.35M per quarter or about 2.8x the very early run rate for sales in the first 5 weeks.

In this article, I will break down why current expectations seem very achievable. In addition, Kura trades at such a discount relative to its cash position that I believe it presents asymmetric upside. If expectations are too high, the company's cash balance presents investors with a substantial margin of safety; however, if the company out-performs expectations there is significant upside to the average analyst price target of $27.75/share.

Therefore, I reiterate my Strong Buy rating for shares of Kura Oncology.

Kura Oncology: Q4 2025 Earnings Update

For the fourth quarter of 2025, Kura reported Komzifti sales of $2.1 million, representing the entirety of the company's net product sales. While this is a modest amount, it was only 5 weeks into the commercialization and triggered a massive $135 million milestone payment from Kyowa Kirin. Importantly, Syndax's Revuforj is approved in R/R AML with both NPM1 mutated or KMT2A-rearranged cancers, while Kura is approved in NPM1-m R/R AML only.

Syndax identified the R/R AML KMT2A patient population as representing ~2,000 patients annually, while NPM1-m patients are ~4,500 annually. Syndax's Revuforj was approved later for NPM1-m patients on October, 24th 2025. Thus, Kura's approval in NPM1-m only lagged Syndax by approximately 3 weeks, coming on November 13th.

On the earnings call, Kura provided qualitative updates from the commercial launch, noting that within 90 days of approval 84% of private payers had established coverage aligned with the label without restrictions. This was described as: "surpass[ing] both industry benchmarks and our internal expectations."

Therefore, the competition for patients in NPM1-m R/R AML may be closer than the market believes. An optimistic trajectory for Komzifti is that it may equal the launch velocity of Revuforj, if Syndax and Kura split the larger patient population in R/R NPM1-m AML equally. The table below shows quarterly sales for Revuforj since its initial FDA approval on November 15th 2024.

Total in 2025 $124.8M --
Syndax Sales by Quarter Revuforj Net Revenue Launch Context
Q4 2024 $7.7M Initial 5 weeks of US launch
Q1 2025 $20.0M First Full Quarter of Sales
Q2 2025 $28.6M 43% QoQ Growth
Q3 2025 $32.0M 12% QoQ Growth
Q4 2025 $44.2M 38% QoQ Growth - NPM1-m Patient Population Unlocked

*Data from Syndax Press Releases. Q4 2025 saw the launch in R/R NPM1-m AML, fueling additional growth.

In order to meet expectations in 2026, Kura needs a launch that is roughly half the magnitude of Syndax's launch to achieve sales of $61.4M. Hypothetically, if Kura and Syndax split 4,500 NPM1-m patients equally they would each realize additional sales of ~$120M assuming equivalent market penetration. Therefore, the market is presently assuming that Syndax will outsell Kura by roughly 3:1.

NPM1-m and KMT2A-r patients account for roughly 50% of the available market in AML, or ~11,000 of the 22,010 cases recorded in the US each year. One reason to remain bullish here is that the overall market is so large.

Kura's management believes that R/R NPM1-m AML has a total addressable market of $350M to $400M in the US. At the mid-point ($375M), this implies a market penetration of 16.4% in the first year.

Syndax's market penetration in the first year of launch in ~2,000 R/R AML KMT2A rearranged patients was very high, reaching $88.3M in sales among these 2,000 patients. This shows how fast the adoption of menin inhibitors has been as the new standard of care in AML.

The lion's share of the AML market potential resides in 1L AML. Both Syndax and Kura are recruiting the pivotal Phase 3 trials, for Kura these are KOMET-017-NIC and IC. In 1,300 patients, these two trials will support the potential approval of Komzifti, requiring at least 2 years.

Kura's core commercial moat was broken down into four pillars: efficacy, compatibility, simplicity and safety. Primarily, Komzifti differentiates itself through safety and simplicity.

In these areas, Komzifti is the first and only once-daily oral option, boasting a significantly cleaner safety profile that entirely avoids the Boxed Warning for QTc prolongation that burdens Syndax. Further, a lack of clinically meaningful drug-drug interactions makes it compatible with patients who may be on multiple drugs simultaneously.

Ultimately, this blend of safety and simplicity should lower the barrier to near-term prescriber adoption in the R/R setting, while also positioning Komzifti as to capture share in the massive frontline market.

Kura Oncology: Analyst Consensus Expectations in 2026 and Beyond

Looking ahead, management estimates the initial US market for NPM1-m R/R AML to be $350M to $400M annually. As discussed, the current analyst consensus for Kura's 2026 total revenue sits at $111.4M.

Because Kura provided guidance for 2026 collaboration revenue of $45M to $55M, the commercial burden is significantly lower. Using the $50M midpoint, Kura needs to generate $61.4 million in net product sales this year to meet analyst expectations. Against a TAM midpoint of $375 million, Kura only needs to secure roughly 16% market penetration in its first full launch year.

If Kura can accomplish this and continue on to achieve $1.23B in sales by 2030 the earnings power will be immense. Even with profits split by the collaboration, analysts see nearly $5/share in earnings for a stock currently trading at $8/share. Clearly, if the consensus is correct shares will need to re-rate to a far higher price.

As commercialization progresses, the narrative will expand from R/R market share to combinations. Relapse rates in AML remain high, with up to 70% relapsing within 3 years. Thus, oncologists rely heavily on combination regimens, and Kura is aggressively positioning Komzifti as a foundational combination partner. The company expects to present updated intensive chemotherapy data from the KOMET-007 trial in the first half of 2026. Furthermore, because FLT3 co-mutations represent a significant portion of the AML population, Kura is actively evaluating Komzifti in combination with the FLT3 inhibitors gilteritinib in the relapsed/refractory setting and quizartinib in the frontline setting.

Because it is impossible to know in advance how this will play out, I prefer to have basket exposure to both Kura and Syndax, even if Syndax has been anointed the market leader based on stock price.

Conclusion

While investors could look at the massive revenue realized by Syndax as evidence that Kura has lost the menin inhibitor race, the reality is that the race has only just begun. As yet, investors only have a glimpse of how this will unfold based on 5 weeks of sales achieved in Q4 2025. In addition, Syndax's expansion to R/R NPM1-m AML only beat Kura to market by the slimmest of margins.

Kura is armed with a differentiated, once-daily drug that explicitly avoids the severe dual-black-box cardiac warnings of its competitor. Furthermore, with nearly $670 million in cash shielding its sub-$725 million market cap, the enterprise value of the company is barely above zero. Wall Street has set a 2026 revenue target that requires Kura to capture just 16% of the addressable market, which may prove a low hurdle for a differentiated asset with access to a deep-pocketed partner in Kyowa Kirin.

At current levels, Kura offers asymmetric upside. If the commercial launch stumbles, the massive cash position provides a substantial margin of safety. But if Kura captures even a modest slice of the market, the stock is primed for a significant re-rating toward the analyst average price target of $27.75 in the coming years.

Therefore, I reiterate my Strong Buy rating.

data.title: Booking Holdings: The Reverse Split Creates Opportunity
data.description:
Co-authored by Kody's Dividends

Many investors fear the term "reverse split."

Usually, a reverse split occurs more often in the fund space or with smaller companies when the share value of the company has declined so much that the company must consolidate the shares outstanding to boost per-share prices. The value of the company itself does not change, just the share price per share. If you imagine a pie, each slice of the pie gets bigger, even though the total pie itself remains the same.

For some companies, however, the value of each individual share can become so large that it causes many investors to no longer be interested in buying shares of the company. Berkshire Hathaway (BRK.A)(BRK.B) is famous for having exceptionally expensive individual shares. Their Class A shares trade hands for over $700,000, which effectively locks out the vast majority of retail investors from owning multiple shares. Some companies, when their share price becomes large enough that they feel that it is pricing out investors they would like to have access to their shares, will enact the opposite of a reverse split. They will enact a share split. This will turn one share into multiple shares. The same story with the pie exists. The pie remains the same size. It's cut into more slices. The idea is that more individual retail investors can enjoy a piece of the pie because it's no longer too expensive.

Today, I want to take a look at a company that is excellent in many regards, but its share price has kept common retail investors out of share ownership. That changes today with a 25-for-1 split that just occurred.

Let's dive in!

An Ecosystem That Supports Long-Term Compounding

With a supply chain of more than 4 million accommodation properties in 220+ countries and territories, Booking Holdings (BKNG) is the largest online travel agency in the world. It's known for leading brands, such as the eponymous Booking.com, Priceline, Kayak, Agoda, and OpenTable.

As we highlighted in our January article, we believe that BKNG can keep growing at a healthy clip in the years ahead. That's because it operates as the leader in a total global travel market that's worth over $3 trillion. This includes a nearly $1 trillion accommodations market, a $900 billion food and beverage market, a $750 billion air market, a $500 billion ground transportation market, and an attractions market worth $300 billion.

In the new economy, travel, experiences, and making memories are viewed by most consumers as more impactful than material possessions. A Euromonitor report released earlier this month found that the shift toward experiences is emerging as a structural change in the global economy. One in two (50%) of global consumers now state they would rather spend money on experiences than on physical possessions. As a result, many consumers are increasingly prioritizing travel over material goods.

BKNG's Q4 2025 results, reported on February 18th, provide tangible proof of this structural shift. Even with a maturing travel market, the company's total revenue surged 16% higher year-over-year (11% on a constant currency basis) to $6.35 billion in the quarter. That beat Seeking Alpha's analyst consensus during the quarter by $216 million.

Travel showed no signs of a slowdown to close out 2025, with travelers booking 285 million room nights in Q4. This 9% increase in room nights over the year-ago period even exceeded management's high-end guidance for the quarter.

Better yet, consumers overall spent more per trip, with gross bookings rising 16% (11% in constant currency) to $43 billion in the fourth quarter. This is largely due to BKNG's Connected Trip strategy (this part of the business grew at a high-20% clip in 2025, and made up a low double-digit percentage of total transactions), which is helping it to capture a larger slice of the $3 trillion global travel ecosystem.

In recent years, the company has prioritized scaling its flight vertical. That's because booking flights is generally the first thing that travelers book. This then allows BKNG to attach high-margin hotel and car rental cross-sells.

The company's adjusted EPS climbed 17.4% higher year-over-year to $48.80 during the fourth quarter. This topped the analyst consensus for the quarter by $0.13. That was driven by a combination of a relatively stable non-GAAP profit margin (24.8%) and a 2.9% reduction in BKNG's weighted average diluted share count in the quarter.

As the company continues to scale its Connected Trip business, this should allow incremental expansion in its profit margins. What's more, BKNG's CFO Ewout Steenbergen noted in his opening remarks during the Q4 2025 Earnings Call that the company anticipates delivering in-year savings of $500 million to $550 million in 2026 via its Transformation Program.

BKNG is also a financially sound enterprise, with an A- S&P credit rating and a stable outlook. Along with its strong free cash flow, this provides it with the flexibility to continue significant share repurchases. For these reasons, the FAST Graphs analyst consensus is for adjusted EPS to compound by 17.1% annually through 2028, off a 2025 base of $228.06.

BKNG's 0.9% dividend yield offers modest starting income. What's most important, though, is that the company has plenty of room to hike the payout further. This is evidenced by the fact that BKNG's adjusted EPS payout ratio is expected to be in the mid-teens in 2026. That should allow it to build on its 2-year dividend growth streak with 10%+ annual dividend growth in the years ahead (for a Chowder number of roughly 11).

Also of note, BKNG's 25:1 stock split approved by the board alongside its Q4 results is set to take effect today. For those who like to avoid fractional shares, this will make it easier to buy whole shares.

From the current $4210.32 share price (as of April 1st, 2026), the stock is trading at a forward 12-month PE ratio of 15.72. For more context, this is well under the 10-year average PE ratio of 25.9, per FAST Graphs.

Moving forward, we believe that a rational fair value PE ratio for BKNG is 22. This would be a bit more than a standard deviation below the 10-year average multiple.

Fundamentally, BKNG's growth profile is as strong now as it has been in recent years. The company's 17.1% annual adjusted EPS growth consensus is higher than the 10-year CAGR of 14.6%.

Thus, our fair value multiple is conservative in that it's already pricing in mild to moderate AI disruption. That signals BKNG is the epitome of an asymmetric risk-reward slant, with shares priced 27% below our fair value per share estimate of $6,089.

If it returns to fair value and matches growth forecasts, the stock could post a 64% total return by the end of 2027.

Now, BKNG does face risks that could set back its investment thesis. These include the long-term risk of margin pressures from the likes of Alphabet (GOOGL)(GOOG) and rising recession risk.

Last November, Google announced that it was rolling out AI Mode and Canvas planning tools. These features allow travelers to generate full itineraries and search for real-time flight deals in 200 countries. What's more, the company has introduced agentic capabilities to enable Gemini to complete tasks, such as comparing hotel amenities and initiating reservations.

While the risk of Google bypassing BKNG altogether is low (Google has repeatedly stated it has no intention of becoming an OTA, and it needs BKNG's huge backend infrastructure and global supply chain to fulfill requests), there is a risk to the latter's margins. That's because Gemini is fundamentally transforming how traffic is sent to BKNG.

Whereas the old funnel was a Google search, clicking on a Booking.com ad, and searching/booking on Booking.com, the new funnel is likely to be talking to Gemini, it finds a room through BKNG's API, the user confirms in the chat, and BKNG processes the payment in the background. In such cases, BKNG loses its front-door brand identity. That could eventually prompt Google to charge more for it at any time, which would at least somewhat crimp profit margins.

In February, Marriott International (MAR) became the first major hotel to recently launch a direct-booking integration with Google's AI mode. This allows users to skip the commissions that they would typically pay to BKNG.

Still, the mitigation factor is that millions of independent apartments, boutique hotels, and villas that comprise most of BKNG's high-margin inventory are too fragmented to do so, which is why Google needs a player like BKNG to access this part of the market.

Another risk to the company is the potential for surging crude oil prices to pressure the discretionary income of consumers at the grocery store and at the pump. Since the U.S. and Israel began strikes on Iran, Kalshi's betting odds for a U.S. recession have jumped from 21.6% to 32.5%. Even if this doesn't come to pass, travel demand will likely be reduced in the near term.

Conclusion

BKNG is a company that is focused on helping you enjoy your travel. They do so by enabling countless merchants offering their wares to be connected to millions of consumers looking for exactly those products. Because it does the work of a middleman, it doesn't need a lot of assets. It's not owning the hotels, it doesn't own the airlines, and it doesn't own the experiences. It simply offers the opportunity to connect those two together for you. This exposes them to a unique set of opportunities and risks. However, BKNG is well-positioned to provide the infrastructure needed for continued travel reservations from different access points.

BKNG is focused on growing its dividends and actively buying back its shares to reward shareholders. The recent stock split enables more shares to be available at lower overall prices, but the value of the company and its value proposition remains the same. Furthermore, it enables the company to continue to buy back shares from investors who may want to trim a position but didn't want to sell a $4000 piece of their portfolio all at once. The goal of a stock split is to create more volume and opportunities for additional investors to buy into the company. Often, a stock split can help a company's overall value rise more rapidly because it enables more interest from more parties. At the end of the day, my portfolio is looking for strong total returns, both from growing dividends and from climbing share values. I don't have an interest in buying a company that is not going to provide me with strong total returns over the long run. I am a long-term buy-and-hold style investor looking for total returns over the long run and am willing to accept sell-offs because of long-term return expectations in the end. This is the classic type of holding for that scenario for a true Dividend King-style investor.

Ignore the chaos of sentiment; find clarity.

data.title: Veralto: Quality Play Is Gradually Winning Me Over
data.description:
Shares of Veralto (VLTO) have fallen quite a bit in the first quarter, despite a resilient end to 2025. The company continues to perform a balancing act between organic growth, M&A, and buybacks, which provide an upward lift to the fundamental performance. This, in combination with falling prices, makes earnings multiples rapidly come down, with appeal being on the increase here.

Frankly, I like the moves made by Veralto, as recent bolt-on dealmaking comes at a price. While shares have come down quite a bit, I like the lower expectations, but it requires a slightly larger pullback before pulling the trigger here to get involved.

Other, higher conviction ideas, including recent M&A efforts, can be found at Value In Corporate Events.

A Bolt-On Deal To End The Quarter

On the final day of the first quarter, Veralto announced the acquisition of GlobalVision. This Montreal-based business leverages proprietary deterministic technology, which helps pharmaceutical and consumer packaged goods customers with speed to market and packaging compliance regulations.

These solutions will be integrated with Veralto's Esko business to meet these customers in facing ever-stringent requirements regarding time to market, consistent branding, and regulatory requirements.

The business is seen contributing $25 million in sales this year, with EBITDA margins seen around 30%, for a $7.5 million EBITDA number. However, Veralto sees EBITDA rise to $13 million; this is defined as the 2026 adjusted EBITDA plus cost synergies. These synergies are thus seen at $5.5 million, suggesting synergies exceeding 20% of sales!

With a 15 times multiple applied to a $13 million pro forma EBITDA number, the purchase price of $195 million is equal to nearly 8 times sales and 26 times standalone EBITDA, both being rather demanding multiples here. The deal is seen as having a neutral impact on 2026 earnings, with accretion seen to 2027 earnings, although this has not been quantified.

The company furthermore provided an update on the share buyback program. With 3.2 million shares bought back at a combined $300 million price tag, this suggests average purchase prices at $93 and change. This is equal to about 1.3% of the pro forma share count.

Pro Forma Implications

Early in February, Veralto announced its 2025 results, with full-year sales up 6% to $5.50 billion, as non-core sales growth came in just shy of 5%. Growth was spread pretty evenly between a $3.3 billion water quality business and a near $2.2 billion product quality & innovation business. Water quality focuses on product solutions that relate to water scarcity, contamination, and quality, while product quality & innovation is about coloring, packaging, marking, and conditioning.

Already fat non-GAAP operating margins of 24.3% of sales rose another 20 basis points on the year before, with margins reported at similar levels across both segments. Non-GAAP earnings of $977 million were equal to $3.90 per share. The reconciliation to GAAP earnings was both fair and small, with GAAP earnings reported at $3.76 per share.

Net debt is reported at $642 million, which is a very modest number in relation to adjusted operating profits of $1.34 billion. Factoring in the impact of the recent deal and $300 million in buybacks in the first quarter, I see pro forma net debt just over $1.1 billion. That, however, is not all, as the company closed on the acquisition of In-Situ in January, that $435 million deal having been announced in November of last year.

The company guided for 2026 core sales to grow by low- to mid-single digits, which, combined with modest margin expansion, should result in adjusted earnings advancing to $4.10-$4.20 per share.

Trading down to $88 per share, the 250 million shares grant Veralto a $22 billion equity valuation and a $22.7 billion enterprise valuation ahead of the recent capital allocation moves. This values the business at around 4 times sales, with equity trading around 22-23 times trailing earnings; this multiple is seen dropping a turn based on forward earnings.

Of course, the purchase of GlobalVision is only set to boost sales by half a point. However, the deal brings real accretion to margins and likely the organic growth profile of the business as well, although that is too small to make a real dent.

What Now?

I last covered shares of Veralto on the first day of the year, concluding that Veralto was putting some capital to work with the purchase of In-Situ. This was accompanied by the announcement of a $750 million buyback program, 40% of which was completed in the first quarter.

Between that buyback program and two deals closing in the first quarter, pro forma net debt of $1.5 billion is largely in line with EBITDA. Leverage is far from a concern, while a more than 10% pullback to $88 per share makes it so that earnings multiples have fallen by 2-3 times. However, the company first had to deliver on all this, as both bolt-on deals are anticipated to see aggressive synergy targets. These first have to be delivered upon to justify their deals in isolation, but in the grand scheme of things, these impacts are relatively small.

In between shares pulling back and earnings coming in a bit stronger than expected, multiples are coming in. Further growth is seen in 2026, as mid-twenty times earnings multiples have fallen to the low-twenties, which is compelling. Shares are now trading at the lowest levels since the spring of 2024 following the separation from Danaher, as valuation multiples have gradually come down.

This makes me more upbeat here, in between a modest premium valuation multiple applied, moderate leverage employed, and Veralto finding a good balance in between organic growth, a strong positioning, buybacks, and M&A efforts.

This makes shares of the company, which aims to safeguard the world's vital resources, rapidly become a safer place for investors as well. With M&A efforts relying on aggressive synergy targets, and elevated valuation multiples, I am waiting for a slightly larger pullback to the low eighties before gradually buying the dip here.

data.title: Maxeon Solar: Get Out Before Getting Wiped Out - Strong Sell (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Note:

I have covered Maxeon Solar Technologies, Ltd. or "Maxeon Solar" (MAXN) previously, so investors should view this as an update to my earlier articles on the company.

In late 2024, I urged investors to consider selling their shares of beleaguered solar panel manufacturer Maxeon Solar Technologies due to a combination of weak market conditions and persistent detainment of the company's Mexican-manufactured products by U.S. Customs & Border Protection ("CBP").

This turned out to be a good call, as the stock has lost more than 80% of its value over the past fifteen months:

In recent quarters, the company has engaged in a variety of restructuring transactions including divestment of certain of its subsidiaries, as well as the sale of its non-U.S. distributed generation business.

However, proceeds had to be used for covering trade payables, taxes, severance expenses, interest payments and costs associated with the purchase of U.S.-manufactured modules.

While Maxeon has challenged CBP's decision in court, no imminent solution is expected.

CBP's denial of entry for certain of the company's products has also impacted Maxeon's ability to fulfil contractual commitments. In response, several customers have commenced legal actions against the company, alleging breach of contract and seeking more than $70 million in damages.

Adding insult to injury, the development of the company's new Maxeon 8 interdigitated back contact ("IBC") solar cells has suffered a number of setbacks including an unsuccessful collaboration arrangement with its controlling shareholder, Chinese wafer manufacturer TCL Zhonghuan Renewable Energy Technology Co., Ltd. or "TZE". As a result, the company will have to self-fund all further research and development activities.

Maxeon Solar's liquidity issues have escalated in recent weeks. As a result, the company was forced to enter into a $7.9 million assignment agreement for a $14 million license payment expected to be received by April 30 at the latest date.

Unfortunately, things are likely to get much worse for the company's already badly stricken shareholders as Maxeon Solar just filed for the Singapore equivalent of chapter 11:

Following approval by the Company's board of directors, on April 1, 2026, the Company announced that together with its subsidiary, Maxeon Solar Pte Ltd. ("MSPL"), they have each filed a voluntary application with the High Court of the Republic of Singapore (the "Court") to place the Company or MSPL, as the case may be, under (a) judicial management pursuant to section 91 of the Insolvency, Restructuring and Dissolution Act 2018 of Singapore (the "IRDA") (the "JM Applications").

Similar to chapter 11 of the U.S. Bankruptcy Code, judicial management under Singapore law is a court‑ordered corporate restructuring process which aims to provide financially troubled companies time to restructure and rehabilitate its business and/or achieve a better realization of its assets than it would have in a liquidation scenario.

Unlike chapter 11, the restructuring process will be led by external judicial managers:

A separate application was filed pursuant to section 92 of the IRDA (the "IJM Applications") for the appointment of Messrs. Tan and Lim of Deloitte Singapore SR&T Restructuring Services Pte. Ltd. as the joint and several interim judicial managers of the Company and MSPL, pending the substantive hearing of the JM Applications (together with the JM Applications, the "Applications").

Please note that Maxeon is not seeking a scheme of arrangement which would have provided existing management the ability to negotiate an amicable solution with creditors and potentially preserve some limited value for equity holders.

In contrast, judicial management is structurally designed to prioritize creditors above all else with a sale of the company's assets being the most likely outcome at this point.

With Maxeon's more valuable assets already having been monetized in recent quarters and considering the dismal state of the company's remaining business, sales proceeds are likely to be tiny fraction of Maxeon Solar's $350+ million in debt obligations.

As a result, I would expect common shareholders to be wiped out at the end of the court-supervised process.

Please note also that the Singapore insolvency proceedings will likely result in Nasdaq delisting the company's shares within short notice pursuant to Rule 5100.

Given my expectations for a near-term delisting and an ultimate wipe-out for equity holders, I am downgrading Maxeon Solar's common shares from "Sell" to "Strong Sell".

Risk Factors

With Nasdaq delisting looming, I am struggling to come up with major risks to the thesis other than perhaps some kind of dead cat bounce over the next couple of days.

Bottom Line

Maxeon Solar Technologies has succumbed to long-standing liquidity issues and applied for being placed under judicial management pursuant to section 91 of the Insolvency, Restructuring and Dissolution Act 2018 of Singapore ("IRDA").

As the company is not pursuing a scheme of arrangement, a sale of Maxeon's remaining assets appears to be the most likely outcome at this point.

With sales proceeds likely to be a fraction of the company's sizeable debt, common equity holders are likely to end up with a total loss.

Given my expectations for a near-term delisting of Maxeon Solar's common shares and an ultimate wipe-out for equity holders, I am downgrading the stock from "Sell" to "Strong Sell".

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Orla Mining: Down Substantially And I'm Buying This Growth Story
data.description:
Orla Mining stock has fallen by 21% from its early March highs, and at today’s roughly $16-a-share price, the valuation is compelling enough for me to consider buying. This is a name I have been watching since the Musselwhite acquisition closed last year, and the pullback is giving investors a chance to buy into a clean growth story at a price that undervalues what this company is becoming.

The setup is straightforward. You have a North American gold producer focused entirely in Canada, the US, and Mexico. Three of the best mining jurisdictions on the planet. Production just crossed 300,000 ounces for the first time, costs are competitive, the balance sheet is in net cash territory, and there is a fully funded development pipeline that adds another 100,000-plus ounces within two years. At a current gold price north of $4,700/oz, the margin expansion story here is hard to ignore.

And yet the stock trades at roughly 9 times its 2026 estimated earnings. I think that is too cheap for what you are getting, as I argue below.

2025 Was The Proof of Concept

The Musselwhite acquisition was the defining move, and it closed in early March 2025. A year later, investors should be thrilled with this move.

Orla paid just $810 million in upfront cash plus $40 million in contingent consideration, to acquire a producing underground gold mine in Ontario from Newmont (NEM). This may have seemed like an expensive deal one year ago, with gold prices at $2,500/oz. But today, Orla is certianly looking like it got the better of the deal as the timing was perfect.

Gold has nearly doubled since the deal was announced. Imagine buying a business that sells a product for $100, and then just a year later, the product sells for $200 (at no additional cost to you). That's essentially what's happened here.

And Musselwhite has gone from a single-asset bolt-on to the company's highest-producing mine. It has been in operation for over 25 years, having produced close to 6 million ounces of gold historically, with a long track record of resource growth and conversion. Think about the math for a second. Orla paid roughly $850 million total for a mine that, at today's gold prices, is probably worth two to three times that. You don't get deals like that often, and this has turned out to be a phenomenal deal for Orla.

Orla had a great 2025, plain and simple. The company exceeded its production guidance, and all-in sustaining costs came in within its guidance range.

The company also generated record free cash flow of $133 million in Q4 alone, on revenue of $378 million. For a company that most investors still think of as a junior, those are mid-tier producer numbers.

A North American Platform With Room To Grow

What separates Orla from a lot of the mid-cap gold names I cover is the jurisdictional profile.

Each one of its producing assets and development projects sits in top-tier mining jurisdictions.

  • Camino Rojo is in Zacatecas, Mexico, with reserves of 760,000 ounces and M&I resources of 5 million ounces across a 139,000-hectare land package.
  • Musselwhite sits on 65,000 hectares in Ontario, with 1.5 million ounces in reserves and 2.4 million in M&I resources.
  • South Railroad, which I will get to in a moment, is on the Carlin Trend in Nevada, arguably the top mining jurisdiction in the world.

You are not taking on Mali risk or Burkina Faso risk or any of the geopolitical uncertainty that plagues a lot of the lower-valued gold names. That should command a premium, and at current prices, it does not.

Musselwhite Has More To Give

Musselwhite has a lot of upside. Based on the current technical report, it only has a mine life through 2030 with expected output of 202,000ozAU per year.

The real upside here is what could come after that. Orla's initial exploration results have confirmed a 2-kilometer extension of the mine trend beyond current operations, and the company is planning 62,500 meters of drilling across 6 rigs in 2026 to evaluate that potential. Also, keep in mind that its processing facility has a nameplate capacity of 1.5 million tonnes per year, but is currently operating at 1.0 million tonnes, leaving excess mill capacity ready to absorb new discoveries without Orla needing to invest additional capital.

For 2026, Musselwhite is guided for 230,000 to 240,000 ounces at AISC as low as $1,650/oz. that's higher than the legacy cost structure, but still very profitable at current gold prices. And if the mine life extension drilling delivers, the real payoff comes in years three through five as Musselwhite evolves from a short-life asset into a long-duration producer.

South Railroad Changes The Trajectory

This is the catalyst that makes Orla a different company by 2028. The updated feasibility study, released in January, confirmed the project's economics at multiple gold prices.

At $3,100 gold, the study shows an after-tax NPV of $783 million and a 48% IRR, with initial capital of $395 million. At spot gold prices closer to $4,700, the NPV soars to well over $1.7 billion with an IRR that makes this one of the best development-stage projects in the sector. Average production over the first five years is 130,000 ounces annually at an AISC of $1,485/oz, scaling to about 104,000 ounces per year over the full 10-year mine life.

Construction spending has already started. The BLM Record of Decision is targeted for mid-2026, with full construction expected to take roughly 18 months from there. That puts first gold pour sometime in early 2028. The whole thing is self-funded through operating cash flow and cash on hand. No equity raise needed.

When South Railroad reaches steady state, Orla expects consolidated production to approach 500,000 ounces. That moves this company firmly into intermediate producer territory and should drive a meaningful re-rating.

Balance Sheet and Shareholder Base

The balance sheet cleaned up faster than I expected. Orla ended 2025 with $421 million in cash against $385 million in debt, putting the company in a net cash position despite having closed an $810 million acquisition less than a year earlier. That is a rapid deleveraging. The company also initiated its first quarterly dividend at $0.015 per share, which is modest but symbolic of the cash flow inflection.

One thing that gives me additional confidence is the shareholder base.

Pierre Lassonde holds an 11.9% stake on a diluted basis. For anyone unfamiliar, Lassonde co-founded Franco-Nevada (FNV) in 1982, one of the most successful gold investments in market history. He is one of the most respected names in the precious metals world. When someone with his track record has that much skin in the game, it tells you something about how he views the risk-reward. Fairfax and Fidelity are also top holders.

Valuation

At around $16 a share, Orla trades at roughly 9.2x 2026 estimated earnings of $1.75. That is cheap for a gold producer with this growth profile and this jurisdictional quality. Looking further out, the six analysts covering the stock expect average EPS of $2.62 by 2028, which puts the 2028 P/E at about 6x based on today's stock price. The expected51% jump in earnings from 2027 to 2028 reflects the contribution from South Railroad coming online.

Compared to peers, Orla offers a combination of below-average valuation, low jurisdictional risk, and above-average production growth that is hard to find elsewhere in the mid-cap gold space. Names like Alamos Gold (AGI) trade at a deserved premium for their Canadian focus, but at 14x forward earnings, the growth is more fully priced in. Equinox Gold (EQX) and B2Gold (BTG) are cheaper, but both carry meaningfully higher geopolitical risk.

Risks

The gold prepay arrangement is one to watch. Orla still has about 105,000 ounces remaining under a gold prepay facility, with deliveries of roughly 4,000 ounces per month through February 2028 at an average price of $2,940. That is well below spot, so every ounce delivered under the prepay is leaving money on the table. It is the cost of funding the Musselwhite acquisition without diluting shareholders, and it was the right call, but it does drag on realized prices in the near term.

Permitting at South Railroad is the other key risk. It should get a record of decision by mid-2026, and while the project has FAST-41 covered status under NEPA, please keep in mind that permitting timelines in Nevada are not always predictable. A delay would push first production into late 2028 or 2029 and could weigh on the stock. Management seems confident, but permits are never a certainty until the paper is signed.

Cost inflation is an issue that has plagued gold miners for years, and remains a key risk - especially now with oil and energy prices soaring as a result of the US-Iran war. The 2026 AISC guidance of $1,550 to $1,750 per ounce is higher than 2025, partly reflecting the inclusion of a full year of Musselwhite and partly reflecting higher costs. At $4,700 gold, margins are still excellent, but a sharp pullback in gold prices would quickly compress them. The operating leverage cuts both ways.

Bottom Line

Orla is transitioning from a junior into an intermediate gold producer, and the market has not fully priced in where this company will be in two years. You have a North American-focused platform with over 300,000 ounces of current production, a self-funded third mine on the Carlin Trend set to come online in 2028, and a balance sheet already in net cash territory. The shareholder base includes Pierre Lassonde and other institutional heavyweights who clearly see the long-term value.

At under $20 a share and roughly 9x forward earnings, I think the current price is a favorable entry point. The pullback from the March highs has given investors a chance to buy into this growth story at a discount to where I believe it should trade.

data.title: A Golden Era For Income Investing - 2 High Yield Picks I Like
data.description:
Introduction

As I write this, there's a ton of uncertainty surrounding the U.S. economy. As someone who was bullish on the overall market, mainly due to us seeing multiple interest rate cuts this year, now I'm not so sure.

However, in chaos comes opportunity. And as long as I can remember, now may be one of the best times to be an income-oriented investor. I've stated previously that investors should have at least 2 portfolios minimum.

And one of those should be solely focused on income. With the likelihood for the market to see muted returns for at least the near to medium-term, this article highlights why now may be a good time to be an income-focused investor, and two high yield picks I like and why.

Uncertainty Rising

In 2025, although we saw uncertainty, the S&P (SP500) still managed to close up over 16%, solid all things considered. The market was led by the Technology (XLK) sector due to AI enthusiasm.

Through the first three plus months of 2026, things have seemed to shift. Now, the market seems to believe the economy may be in worse shape than advertised. Me, personally, I never believed the economy was in all that good of shape.

Mainly due to what I've personally witnessed from family & friends. And going forward, I think things may get worse before they get better. We also are currently at war with Iran. And at the time of writing, Iran has rejected peace talks.

Currently, Iran has laid out 5 conditions for the war to end. The question is will the U.S. & Israel accept. My belief is no. And the longer the war goes on, the worse things will likely get.

At the time of writing, Iran has closed the Strait of Hormuz, causing oil & gas prices to rise. There's also the potential to see the U.S. send in ground troops into Iran. If this happens, I foresee market volatility increasing and potentially entering bear market territory.

This is because inflation fears will increase, dimming rate cut hopes. Not to mention, mortgage rates are back on the rise, resulting in affordability issues for a lot of Americans.

As a result, it's likely the broader market will underperform, or at least muted returns for the near to medium-term. But the two picks I mention stand to benefit from the market's potential underperformance. Without further ado, let's get into the two high yields.

Goldman Sachs S&P 500 Premium Income ETF (GPIX) - 8.81% Yield

The first high yield fund I think is attractive is GPIX by Goldman Sachs (GS). Unlike some of their peers, I like that fact the GPIX actually holds equities instead of using a synthetic strategy to get exposure.

In my opinion, this makes them attractive as a potential long-term holding. GPIX's top 10 holdings include behemoths like NVIDIA (NVDA), Apple (AAPL), Microsoft (MSFT), Broadcom (AVGO), Amazon (AMZN), and Meta Platforms (META).

As a result, Technology (XLK) is their largest sector at 33%. And while tech stocks have underperformed recently, rapid AI adoption will likely benefit GPIX long-term.

The most attractive metric about GPIX and why I think they can potentially outperform is their covered call strategy. And in sideways markets, like now, the fund is able to harness higher options premiums. This is because GPIX only sells options of a portion of their portfolio.

Typically, this is between 25% to 75% of the portfolio, leaving the rest uncovered. This is different than peer, the NEOS S&P High Income ETF (SPYI), who typically sells options on 100% of its assets.

While this allows GPIX to participate in more upside in bull markets, they are still limited due to their covered call strategy. This is why they're likely to underperform.

This also allows them to continue paying a solid distribution as they harness higher options premiums. As a result, they can potentially outperform the index in total returns.

While many covered funds usually see lower distributions in volatile markets due to NAV erosion, this is where GPIX's superior strategy outshines its peers. Year-to-date, the S&P (SP500) is down 3.7% at the time of writing. GPIX is down 4.37%, while SPYI is down 5.17%.

But GPIX's monthly distributions make them a worthy investment in the current environment. In the months of January and February, the ETF paid monthly distributions of $0.3755 and $0.3763. For comparison, during the first 3 months, SPYI distributions declined modestly.

In January, SPYI paid out $0.5309. Next they paid $0.5219 and for March $0.5104. I also like the fact that GPIX has a lower expense ratio at 0.35%. This is compared to 0.68% for SPYI. But SPYI's distribution yield is much higher at 12.04%, compared to 8.81% for GPIX.

Goldman Sachs Nasdaq-100 Premium Income ETF (GPIQ) - 10.95% Yield

Sticking with Goldman Sachs, GPIQ is the second fund I would consider. The only difference between GPIQ and GPIX is the indexes they track. GPIQ follows the Nasdaq (NDAQ), making them more tech heavy.

As a result, the sector makes up roughly 51% of the portfolio. Another key difference is the Communications Services (XLC) sector make up the second largest (sector) instead of the Financials (XLF).

Going forward, GPIQ is likely to outperform long-term as many remain bullish on the rapid growth of AI adoption in the U.S. Because they follow the NDAQ, GPIQ only has 105 holdings compared to 499 for GPIX.

GPIQ has the same expense ratio at 0.35% and uses a dynamic options strategy, selling options on a portion of the portfolio. This allows for greater upside participation in bull markets.

But as previously mentioned, the funds are likely to underperform in bull markets. But in down or flat markets, this is where both funds are designed to shine as they are able to generate higher options premiums.

Year-to-date, GPIQ has fared better than NEOS' Nasdaq-100 High Income ETF (QQQI), who's down over 6%. JPMorgan's Nasdaq Equity Premium Income ETF (JEPQ) has seen less of a drawdown compared to both.

As far as distributions, JEPQ has seen theirs decline modestly. In January, the ETF paid a monthly distribution of $0.5761. In February, this was $0.4657 and increased in March to $0.5090.

QQQI's declined on sequential basis from $0.6359 in January, $0.6140 in February, and $0.6089 in March. While this may not seem significant, it can be for someone strictly focused on income.

For comparison, GPIQ's distributions declined month over month from $0.4655 in February to $0.4527 in March. For January, their distribution in $0.4633.

Why These Two Picks

Because of uncertainty increasing, investors may be wondering why these two funds since the S&P and Nasdaq are both down YTD. Particularly, the NDAQ is down double-digits, close to 14%.

Because of their options strategies leaving a portion of their portfolios uncovered in bull markets, I believe this makes GPIX & GPIQ superior funds for long-term holdings. They also have lower expense ratios than most peers, which can eat into an investor's returns long-term.

You can see from the chart below that both GPIX & GPIQ outperformed some of their peers in total returns in 2025. Only SPYI was able to outperform GPIX, while GPIQ outperformed both QQQI & JEPQ.

While past performance is no indicator of future performance, I think the market uncertainty is likely to last a while longer. This is mainly because of the ongoing war. And having an income-focused portfolio can blunt the blow from the potential to see underperformance in the overall market.

Plus you can collect higher yields that deliver reliable and stable income. Which is essential during periods of uncertainty like now. Both GPIX and GPIQ have also seen stable NAV growth due to their lower distribution yields.

While some investors may prefer funds with significantly higher yields, GPIX & GPIQ's superior covered call strategies make them ideal for holding long-term, not just during periods of uncertainty.

And lastly, because they both use 1256 contracts, both funds' distributions are tax favorable. The chart below shows how their distributions were taxed for fiscal year 2025. GPIQ had a higher amount of their distributions considered return of capital at close to 96%. GPIX's distributions were approximately 88% ROC.

Risks & Conclusion

Although I consider GPIX and GPIQ superior funds, both are not immune to underperformance.

As you can see from above, both are also down YTD due to increased volatility. And while both were designed to outperform in down or flat markets, a longer war has the potential to push us into a recession.

If so, both funds' yields will likely not save them from seeing negative total returns. This is something investors just be aware of going forward.

But if you're someone looking to position your portfolio to deliver reliable income during market volatility and hold long-term, then I believe GPIX & GPIQ are the best positioned funds for the job.

data.title: PVH: Setup Is Not Good Enough To Justify An Upgrade To Buy
data.description:
Investment summary

My previous investment thought on PVH Corp. (PVH) was a hold rating because I was concerned about the near-term earnings pressure given the tariff situation, lack of revenue growth, and geopolitical risk in China. In this update, I still think a neutral stance makes sense. The positive is that PVH’s turnaround work looks more credible now, with better brand execution and stronger product campaigns. The issue is that underlying growth is still not strong enough, and tariff headwinds remain visible.

4Q25 results update

PVH reported Q4 2025 revenue of $2.5 billion, which was a 6% y/y growth on a reported basis but flat in constant currency [CC]. By region, EMEA revenue grew 8% on a reported basis but fell 3% CC; Americas revenue grew 4%; and APAC revenue was flat on a reported basis and down 2% CC. One thing to note is that the APAC result included a ~4% decline from the Lunar New Year timing shift as the holiday fell in Q4 2024 but not in Q4 2025. In terms of brand performance, Tommy Hilfiger revenue grew 7% reported and 1% CC; Calvin Klein revenue grew 3% reported but fell 1% CC. By channel, direct-to-consumer [DTC] revenue grew 1% reported and fell 3% CC, while owned and operated digital commerce revenue grew 5% reported and was flat CC. Wholesale revenue did better with 11% reported growth and 4% CC. However, growth did not translate into bottom line growth directly. Adj. EPS was only up slightly from $3.27 to $3.82. The main reason was the gross margin decline due to higher tariffs on goods entering the US, the transition of previously licensed women’s categories, and a more promotional environment.

The turnaround progress

Previously, my positive view was that PVH's brand-building actions were starting to work and that the Calvin Klein operating issues were getting resolved. Those notions continue to advance in the right direction as management is now talking about on-time deliveries, margins back on plan at Calvin Klein, stronger spring sell-through across both brands and all three regions, and a third consecutive season of positive order books in Europe. Relative to things a few quarters ago, this is a much cleaner story, in my view.

Take Calvin Klein, for instance. The core products (underwear and denim) continue to see good demand, but importantly, the newer product and campaign work seems to be landing better. One of these newer initiatives is Icon Cotton Stretch, which grew 20% in men’s and 13% in women’s. Another example is fashion denim, which management noted is now >50% of Calvin’s denim business and grew high single digits. Besides the positive growth numbers, which are great, I think the key takeaway here is that PVH is able to pair its core products with major talent (i.e., famous people) and strong marketing to capture more demand. The best supporting evidence is the Jungkook campaign, which drove sales of campaign items up >50% after launch, and the jackets he wore reached 60% sell-through in two weeks.

We see a similar pattern in Tommy Hilfiger. The brand saw strong growth in the cable-knit sweater franchise, with sales up >50% in Q4. Similarly to Calvin Klein, where PVH leveraged a single talent, the Tommy Hilfiger brand leveraged the Liverpool brand (a partnership), which drove a 200% increase in sales for tunnel-walk products in Europe vs. the previous week. The same was seen in China, where Tommy DTC sales were up double digits in March after local activations around the Formula 1 partnership.

The common pattern between the two is that PVH will focus on core categories, create visible brand moments (leveraging an existing entity that has a strong brand), and turn those moments into traffic and sell-through. Importantly, PVH has been successfully repeating this strategy, and that gives comfort that PVH is in the growth driver seat now.

The setup is better, but it is still not clean enough for a bullish call

That said, the setup is not entirely clean. The first problem is growth quality. As much as I see a repeatable strategy that is promising, Q4 revenue was flat CC. Calvin Klein was even down 1% CC, and DTC was also down 3% CC. Across regions, EMEA was also down 3% and APAC was down 2% CC. Americas did perform better, but remember that it benefited from taking previously licensed women’s categories in-house. I need to see actual growth accelerate (CC basis) in order to be convinced that PVH’s strategy is indeed working to drive growth.

The second problem is that the next leg of the story still depends on external factors' pressure easing. Even if PVH continues to execute really well, the tariffs' impact needs to go away in order for the P&L to reflect that success. But that is not the case, apparently. Management is guiding to ~215 bps gross tariff impact in 2026, with a gross EBIT hit of ~$195 million, which translates to ~$3.30 per share, before mitigation. They did say to mitigate ~60% of that this year and exit the year at >75% mitigation on an annualized basis, but the key word is annualized. Which means this could be something they achieved in Q4, and the actual impact would be much more than that 75% implies. So, PVH can do a lot right operationally and still have the reported numbers look weak because the tariff drag is just so big.

Moreover, I am particularly worried about the gap between brand momentum and channel confidence. Management said wholesalers remain cautious and the consumer environment is still not exactly robust. While Europe's order books are positive, which is clearly better than before, in-season replenishment was weak in Q4, which implies wholesalers are not expecting a strong demand ahead. APAC wholesale partners are also still taking a cautious approach as well. This tells me the demand strength is not exactly in the “strong” territory.

Valuation

I still don’t think we are going to see any major upward re-rating to the multiples here. There are tangible changes in core brands, but Q4 growth was really not that strong, and the FY2026 revenue guide basically tells us growth is not going to accelerate hard. Moreover, wholesalers remain cautious, which indicates that underlying demand may not be as strong. PVH's current forward PE is ~6x for a business that is expected to grow its topline by ~low-single-digits, which seems fair when I compare it to Abercrombie & Fitch (ANF), which trades at ~8.6x with mid-single-digit revenue growth expectations.

Conclusion

I am maintaining a hold rating on PVH. I do think the business is moving in the right direction. But I am not ready to get bullish yet. CC growth was still weak in Q4, wholesalers remain cautious, and the tariff impact in 2026 is large enough to weigh on earnings even if execution improves further. For now, I think PVH looks better as a business, but the stock is not that attractive yet.

data.title: Navigating The Hormuz Crisis
data.description:
Listen on the go! A daily podcast of Wall Street Breakfast will be available by 8:00 a.m. on Seeking Alpha, iTunes, Spotify.

Holiday reminder: Wall Street Breakfast won't be published tomorrow, with markets closed for Good Friday.

Good morning! Here's the latest in trending:

Tariff watch: The White House is preparing to overhaul steel and aluminum tariffs, and eyes new levies on certain drugmakers.

Code leak: Anthropic (ANTHRO) is working to contain the leak of some internal source code for its AI agent Claude Code.

Renewed optimism: Recession odds on prediction market Kalshi (KALSHI) plummet after better-than-expected economic data.

Way forward

President Donald Trump has quashed market hopes for a quick end to the Middle East war, saying the U.S. will strike Iran "extremely hard" over the next two to three weeks. That likely means the Strait of Hormuz will continue to remain effectively closed, sending stock markets lower and oil prices higher once again.

Long road ahead: Trump said the U.S. will consider a ceasefire when the Strait of Hormuz is open and clear. "Until then, we are blasting Iran into oblivion or, as they say, back to the Stone Ages!!!" he declared. Iranian Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi said the future of the waterway would be decided jointly by Iran and Oman after the war ends, and not by external powers. "From our point of view, the strait is currently open and is closed only to those who are at war with Iran," Araghchi clarified. Iran has been imposing significant fees, reportedly up to $2M per tanker in some cases, for safe passage, with payments often coordinated in non-U.S. dollar currencies such as Chinese yuan.

Global response: The UK will host a summit of 35 countries to "assess all viable diplomatic and political measures we can take to restore freedom of navigation [in the Strait of Hormuz], guarantee the safety of trapped ships and seafarers, and to resume the movement of vital commodities," UK Prime Minister Keir Starmer said. Meanwhile, Gulf Arab states are considering new export pipelines to bypass the Strait of Hormuz despite high costs and complexities that stalled similar plans in the past. Viable near-term options include the expansion of Saudi Arabia's East-West pipeline and Abu Dhabi's existing route to Fujairah. Another option could be reviving plans for the India-Middle East-Europe Economic Corridor, with Israel's Haifa Port serving as a major gateway.

SA commentary: Investing Group Leader Bret Jensen warned that even if the Strait of Hormuz was reopened fully, the damaged assets in the region could take months – even years – to bring back online. Moreover, the oil reserves that have been released by countries will need to be replenished over time. "I expect disruption to global energy supply to last even after a ceasefire or a peace deal," said SA analyst Geneva Investor. "This disruption cannot be understated, as it can be quantified as almost twice as large as that of the 1973 oil crisis overall." Hormuz Situation Creates 'Strait-Line' Case For BNO Over USO

Here's the latest Seeking Alpha analysis

Back To Bullion: Top Gold Stocks For The Yellow Metal Rebound

Sell The Rally - There Is No Easy Way Out Of Iran War

Amazon: This Is Worse Than You Think

Alphabet's Solution Is A Gift For Micron's Memory Sales

2 Undervalued 8-9% Yields The Market Is Sleeping On After Energy's Huge Rally

What else is happening...

Trump floats NATO exit again as rift with European allies deepens.

Lucas drones, inspired by Iran's tech, signal U.S. strategy shift.

Lilly (LLY) CEO opposes codification of Trump drug pricing deals.
